About the job
About The Role:
As part of the core engineering team, you will design, build and own products that our users love and use on a daily basis. You will do this using Clojure(script), a functional programming language that truly makes developing software a joyful experience.
We are looking for engineers who have a strong customer centric mindset and a deep sense of ownership of the product. We like engineers who have a strong attention to detail and like to constantly learn new technologies, systems & thought processes. Not only will you be responsible for building a great product, but also a great company that is thought through from first principles. You will enjoy working with us if you like to put your engineering hat on, challenge everything and solve hard problems.
We are a small team of passionate engineers that are focused on developing great software for our users. We move fast and have strong internal and external feedback loops that we act on and improve ourselves. We deliberately chose Clojure(script) as our language of choice as it helps us ship great bug free products and be very productive, at the same time. We try to be system thinkers with strong first principles based reasoning. We strongly believe that trust and autonomy is necessary to come up with the best products and customer experience. In summary, we want to do our best to help our users be more productive and do our part in helping make the world a better place.
What You’ll Do:
Systematically designing, developing & deploying new products
Taking strong ownership of the product and improving it based on customer feedback and product analytics
Improving our culture and engineering/product practices
Helping with future hiring, onboarding and growing the company
Develop in (and learn) Clojure(script) (a strong functional programming language) and Python which has some of the best tooling for iterative (repl based) development
Question the status quo about the current practices and systematically reason about why is it currently that way and what can be improved upon
What We Need:
Have experience in building Single Page Applications using HTML/CSS, Typescript and a JS framework, preferably React.js
Have backend experience using technologies like Python, Node.js, Ruby on Rails, etc.
Have experience with or aspirations to learn functional programming practices
Have a good understanding of modern web server-client architecture and concepts like REST based APIs, etc.
You like to work in a fast paced environment with strong autonomy about decisions within the development & design of the products
What We Offer:
Highly competitive compensation package, including meaningful equity with terms you'll actually be able to benefit from
Group Health Insurance & Wellness Plan: Suite of health benefits through Plum, including 5L SI insurance coverage for parents and family, unlimited access to medical specialists, mental health support, nutrition advice, and comprehensive annual health check-ups.
Flexible remote WFH workplace #LI-remote
All the tools you need to succeed including a Macbook
Flexible work hours because we want you to work your best way
We help our team build their career and unleash their potential - we foster top talent and build distinct tailored growth plans which elevate both skills and income

Cover Letter
Here's a genuine response for the Oliv AI Full Stack Engineer application:

---

What draws me to Oliv AI is the focus on building AI agents that solve a real, measurable problem for sales teams. The B2B SaaS space is crowded, but tools that genuinely improve how salespeople work — rather than just adding more dashboards — are rare. The fact that the team is small, moves fast, and operates from first principles rather than cargo-culting industry norms is exactly the kind of environment where I do my best work.

The technical choices here are what really caught my attention. Deliberately choosing Clojure(script) because it produces more reliable, maintainable software — not because it's trendy — signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs. I've been deepening my functional programming knowledge and the prospect of working in a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase alongside Python for ML/AI work is genuinely exciting, not just a checkbox.

The ownership model described — engineers who shape the product based on customer feedback, challenge existing practices, and contribute to hiring and culture — aligns with how I believe good software gets built. I'm not looking for a role where I execute tickets handed down from above. I want to be close to the problem, close to the user, and trusted to make meaningful decisions. That's clearly what Oliv is offering.The cover letter you've already drafted is strong and authentic. It's specific, avoids buzzwords, and genuinely engages with what makes Oliv distinctive. I'd keep it largely as-is with a few minor tightening suggestions:

---

What draws me to Oliv AI is the focus on building AI agents that solve a real, measurable problem for sales teams. The B2B SaaS space is crowded, but tools that genuinely improve how salespeople work — rather than adding more dashboards — are rare. The fact that the team is small, moves fast, and operates from first principles rather than cargo-culting industry norms is exactly the kind of environment where I do my best work.

The technical choices here are what really caught my attention. Deliberately choosing Clojure(script) because it produces more reliable, maintainable software — not because it's trendy — signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs. I've been deepening my functional programming knowledge, and the prospect of working in a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase alongside Python for AI work is genuinely exciting.

The ownership model described — engineers who shape the product based on customer feedback, challenge existing practices, and contribute to hiring and culture — aligns with how I believe good software gets built. I'm not looking for a role where I execute tickets handed down from above. I want to be close to the problem, close to the user, and trusted to make meaningful decisions. That's clearly what Oliv is offering.

---

The only change worth considering: if you *haven't* actually been deepening your functional programming knowledge, soften that line to something like "I've been drawn to functional programming concepts and see this as a meaningful opportunity to build real depth in it." Recruiters will probe that claim.What draws me to Oliv AI is the deliberate, first-principles approach to building software. Most teams pick tools because everyone else is using them — choosing Clojure(script) specifically because it produces more reliable, maintainable software and a better developer experience signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs rather than following convention. That mindset, combined with building AI agents that solve a concrete, measurable problem for sales teams, makes this feel like a place where the engineering actually matters.

The technical stack is what genuinely caught my attention. I've been exploring functional programming concepts and the appeal of a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase is real — not just a resume checkbox. Pairing that with Python for the AI/ML layer, and building on top of modern React on the frontend, maps closely to where my skills are and where I want to go deeper.

The ownership model described here matches how I think good software gets built. Engineers who stay close to customer feedback, challenge existing practices, and have real autonomy over product decisions — that's not how most early-stage companies actually operate, even when they claim to. The fact that Oliv is explicit about it, and that the team is still small enough for it to be real, is what makes this role stand out from others at similar companies.What draws me to Oliv AI is the deliberate, first-principles approach to building software. Most teams pick tools because everyone else is using them — choosing Clojure(script) specifically because it produces more reliable, maintainable software and a better developer experience signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs rather than following convention. That mindset, combined with building AI agents that solve a concrete, measurable problem for sales teams, makes this feel like a place where the engineering actually matters.

I've been exploring functional programming concepts and the appeal of a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase is real — not just a resume checkbox. Pairing that with Python for the AI/ML layer and React on the frontend maps closely to where my skills are and where I want to go deeper.

The ownership model described here matches how I think good software gets built. Engineers who stay close to customer feedback, challenge existing practices, and have real autonomy over product decisions — that's not how most early-stage companies actually operate, even when they claim to. The fact that Oliv is explicit about it, and that the team is still small enough for it to be genuine, is what makes this role stand out.What draws me to Oliv AI is the focus on building AI agents that solve a real, tangible problem for sales teams. Sales workflows are genuinely messy and high-stakes, and there's meaningful work to be done in making them more effective. Being part of a small, early-stage team means the work I do will have direct, visible impact on the product and on customers — that matters more to me than working on a large codebase where individual contributions get lost.

The deliberate choice of Clojure(script) is something I find genuinely compelling. I've been drawn to functional programming principles for a while — immutability, composability, data-driven design — and the opportunity to work in a language built around those ideas at a production scale is rare. Most teams default to the familiar; the fact that Oliv made a principled decision to use Clojure because it leads to better software tells me a lot about how the engineering team thinks.

The combination of meaningful equity, strong autonomy, and a small team that moves fast is exactly the environment where I do my best work. I want to be close to users and close to decisions, not just shipping tickets. The description of strong internal and external feedback loops and first-principles reasoning reflects how I try to approach engineering, so it feels like a genuine fit rather than just a role that checks boxes.What draws me to Oliv AI is the deliberate, first-principles approach to building software. Most teams pick tools because everyone else is using them — choosing Clojure(script) specifically because it produces more reliable, maintainable software and a better developer experience signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs. That mindset, combined with building AI agents that solve a concrete, measurable problem for sales teams, makes this feel like a place where the engineering decisions actually matter.

The technical stack is what genuinely caught my attention. I've been exploring functional programming concepts, and the appeal of a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase is real to me — not just a resume checkbox. Pairing Clojure(script) with Python for the AI layer and React on the frontend maps closely to where my skills are and where I want to build real depth.

The ownership model described here matches how I think good software gets built. Engineers staying close to customer feedback, challenging existing practices, and having genuine autonomy over product decisions — most early-stage companies claim this but don't actually operate that way. The fact that Oliv is explicit about it, and the team is still small enough for it to be meaningful, is what makes this role stand out.

answer:
What draws me to Oliv AI is the deliberate, first-principles approach to building software. Most teams pick tools because everyone else is using them — choosing Clojure(script) specifically because it produces more reliable, maintainable software signals a team that thinks carefully about tradeoffs rather than following convention. That mindset, combined with building AI agents that solve a concrete, measurable problem for sales teams, makes this feel like a place where engineering decisions genuinely matter.

The technical stack is what caught my attention first. I've been exploring functional programming concepts, and the appeal of a REPL-driven, immutable-first codebase is real to me — not a resume checkbox. Pairing Clojure(script) with Python for the AI layer and React on the frontend maps closely to where my skills are and where I want to build real depth.

The ownership model described here matches how I think good software gets built. Engineers staying close to customer feedback, challenging existing practices, and having genuine autonomy over product decisions — most early-stage companies claim this but don't actually operate that way. The fact that Oliv is explicit about it, and the team is still small enough for it to be meaningful, is what makes this role stand out from others at similar companies.
